description: Trust Services Criteria, Security category. Applies to every SOC 2 report regardless of scope — Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, and Privacy are additional, optional categories layered on top of this.

categories.title: Control Environment
categories.summary: Sets the tone at the top: does the org actually value integrity, ethics, and accountability, or just say it does on paper.

controls.criteria: Commitment to integrity and ethical values (code of conduct, standards of conduct communicated to employees/contractors).
controls.notes: Have a written code of conduct that's actually distributed and acknowledged, not just filed away. Auditors want signed acknowledgments, not the policy PDF alone.

controls.criteria: Board (or equivalent oversight body) is independent from management and oversees the design/operation of internal controls.
controls.notes: Org chart showing security/compliance reporting lines with real independence from the teams they oversee.

controls.criteria: Management establishes structures, reporting lines, and appropriate authorities/responsibilities.
controls.notes: Documented org chart and job descriptions tied to security responsibilities.

controls.criteria: Commitment to attracting, developing, and retaining competent individuals.
controls.notes: Background-check and onboarding process, role-specific training records.

controls.criteria: Holds individuals accountable for their internal control responsibilities (including enforcement/discipline).
controls.notes: Documented disciplinary process for policy violations, tied to performance management.

categories.title: Communication and Information
categories.summary: Are policies communicated, and does information flow well enough internally and externally to support the control environment.

controls.criteria: Obtains/generates and uses relevant, quality information to support internal control.
controls.notes: Reliable systems/reporting for security-relevant data (asset inventory, access logs, etc.).

controls.criteria: Internally communicates information, including objectives and responsibilities for internal control.
controls.notes: A living security policy set and a comms cadence — security awareness training counts.

controls.criteria: Communicates with external parties (customers, vendors, regulators) about matters affecting internal control.
controls.notes: Trust center, security page, or documented process for responding to customer security questionnaires.

categories.title: Risk Assessment
categories.summary: Does the org actually identify and analyze risk, including risk from change (new vendors, new systems, growth, fraud).

controls.criteria: Specifies objectives clearly enough to enable risk identification.
controls.notes: Documented business/security objectives that risks can be measured against.

controls.criteria: Identifies risks to objectives and analyzes them as a basis for how to manage them.
controls.notes: Annual risk assessment with defined methodology (likelihood x impact), risk register, treatment plan.

controls.criteria: Considers potential for fraud in assessing risk.
controls.notes: Explicit fraud risk consideration in the risk assessment, not just external threat modeling.

controls.criteria: Identifies and assesses changes that could significantly impact the system of controls.
controls.notes: Change-triggered reassessment process — new product, acquisition, major vendor swap.

categories.title: Monitoring Activities
categories.summary: Does the org check that its own controls are actually working, on an ongoing basis, and fix what's found.

controls.criteria: Selects, develops, and performs ongoing/separate evaluations of whether controls are present and functioning.
controls.notes: Internal control testing/self-audits between external audits.

controls.criteria: Evaluates and communicates control deficiencies timely to those responsible for corrective action.
controls.notes: Defined deficiency-tracking process (ticket/register) with owners and due dates, not a lost Slack message.

categories.title: Control Activities
categories.summary: Are the actual controls, technologies, and policies in place designed to reduce risk to an acceptable level.

controls.criteria: Selects and develops control activities that mitigate risk to acceptable levels.
controls.notes: Every risk from the CC3 risk register should trace to a specific control.

controls.criteria: Selects and develops general controls over technology.
controls.notes: Technology-level controls: access management systems, logging infrastructure, etc.

controls.criteria: Deploys control activities through policies that establish expectations and procedures that put policies into action.
controls.notes: Every policy should have a corresponding real-world procedure and evidence trail.

categories.title: Logical and Physical Access Controls
categories.summary: The big one — most of your actual security controls live here: encryption, access provisioning/deprovisioning, physical security, authentication.

controls.criteria: Implements logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets.
controls.notes: MFA everywhere, encryption at rest and in transit.

controls.criteria: Registers and authorizes new internal/external users prior to granting access; removes access when no longer needed.
controls.notes: Documented access-request/approval/termination workflow with evidence (tickets, HRIS-triggered deprovisioning).

controls.criteria: Role-based access, least privilege, segregation of duties.
controls.notes: Quarterly access reviews with documented sign-off.

controls.criteria: Restricts physical access to facilities and protected information assets.
controls.notes: Badge logs or equivalent for physical spaces.

controls.criteria: Discontinues logical/physical protections over disposed assets.
controls.notes: Documented asset decommissioning/wipe process.

controls.criteria: Protects against threats from outside system boundaries (firewalls, IDS/IPS, etc.).
controls.notes: Perimeter security controls with logging.

controls.criteria: Restricts movement of data to authorized users and processes.
controls.notes: Data loss prevention / egress controls.

controls.criteria: Prevents/detects unauthorized/malicious software.
controls.notes: Endpoint protection with logging.

categories.title: System Operations
categories.summary: Detecting, responding to, and recovering from security events.

controls.criteria: Detects and monitors for new vulnerabilities and configuration changes.
controls.notes: Vulnerability scanning on a defined cadence with tracked remediation SLAs.

controls.criteria: Monitors system components for anomalies indicative of security events.
controls.notes: SIEM/log monitoring with alerting.

controls.criteria: Evaluates security incidents to determine if they need a response.
controls.notes: Documented triage criteria/severity levels.

controls.criteria: Responds to identified security incidents (containment, mitigation, communication).
controls.notes: Written incident response plan, tabletop-tested — auditors want evidence of the test, not just the plan.

controls.criteria: Recovers from identified security incidents (root cause, remediation, restoration).
controls.notes: Post-incident/lessons-learned process.

categories.title: Change Management
categories.summary: Do changes to infrastructure, data, and software go through a controlled process.

controls.criteria: Authorizes, designs, develops/acquires, configures, documents, tests, approves, and implements changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures.
controls.notes: Change management policy with required approvals, mandatory testing before production deploy, dev/test/prod segregation, rollback procedures. CI/CD pipeline logs and PR approval history are usually your evidence.

categories.title: Risk Mitigation
categories.summary: Business continuity and vendor/third-party risk management.

controls.criteria: Identifies, selects, and develops risk mitigation activities for business disruptions.
controls.notes: Business continuity/disaster recovery plan with defined RTO/RPO, evidence of annual DR test.

controls.criteria: Assesses and manages risk associated with vendors and business partners.
controls.notes: Tiered vendor risk management program; signed vendor security questionnaires/SOC 2 reports on file for critical vendors.

categories.title: Availability
categories.summary: Whether the system is available for operation and use as committed or agreed. Optional — relevant if your contracts/SLAs promise uptime.

controls.criteria: Maintains, monitors, and evaluates current processing capacity and use of system components to manage capacity demand and enable the implementation of additional capacity to help meet objectives.
controls.notes: Track resource utilization (CPU/memory/storage/bandwidth) against defined thresholds, with a documented process for scaling before you hit them — auto-scaling policies and capacity-planning reviews count as evidence.

controls.criteria: Authorizes, designs, develops or acquires, implements, operates, approves, maintains, and monitors environmental protections, software, data backup processes, and recovery infrastructure to meet objectives.
controls.notes: Redundant power/cooling for on-prem, multi-AZ/region architecture for cloud, automated backup jobs with defined retention, and a documented disaster-recovery architecture.

controls.criteria: Tests recovery plan procedures supporting system recovery to meet objectives.
controls.notes: An annual (at minimum) DR/failover test with a written report — auditors want evidence the test actually happened and what was fixed afterward, not just a plan that's never been exercised.

categories.title: Confidentiality
categories.summary: Whether information designated as confidential is protected as committed or agreed. Optional — relevant if you handle client data, IP, or other sensitive info beyond PII.

controls.criteria: Identifies and maintains confidential information to meet the entity's objectives related to confidentiality.
controls.notes: A data classification policy that actually labels what's "confidential" (contracts, source code, customer data), with access/handling controls tied to that classification.

controls.criteria: Disposes of confidential information to meet the entity's objectives related to confidentiality.
controls.notes: Documented secure-deletion/data-destruction procedures (crypto-shredding, certified wipe, physical destruction) with retention schedules that actually get enforced, not just written down.

categories.title: Processing Integrity
categories.summary: Whether system processing is complete, valid, accurate, timely, and authorized to meet objectives. Optional — matters most for systems that transform, calculate, or transact on data (billing, payments, reporting).

controls.criteria: Obtains or generates, uses, and communicates relevant, quality information regarding processing objectives, including data definitions and product/service specifications.
controls.notes: Documented data dictionaries/specs for what your system is supposed to process and produce — this is the baseline the rest of PI is tested against.

controls.criteria: Implements policies and procedures over system inputs, including controls over completeness and accuracy, to result in products, services, and reporting that meet objectives.
controls.notes: Input validation, schema checks, required-field enforcement, and reconciliation against source data.

controls.criteria: Implements policies and procedures over system processing to result in products, services, and reporting that meet objectives.
controls.notes: Automated testing of business logic/calculations, error-handling and exception queues, and monitoring for processing failures.

controls.criteria: Implements policies and procedures to make available or deliver output completely, accurately, and timely in accordance with specifications to meet objectives.
controls.notes: Delivery confirmation, output reconciliation, and SLAs for when processed data/reports are due.

controls.criteria: Implements policies and procedures to store inputs, items in processing, and outputs completely, accurately, and timely in accordance with system specifications to meet objectives.
controls.notes: Data integrity checks (checksums, audit trails) and retention controls so stored records match what was actually processed.

categories.title: Privacy
categories.summary: Governs the proper collection, use, retention, disclosure, and disposal of personal information (PII), in line with privacy notices and principles. 18 criteria across 8 privacy categories — optional, selected based on your business model.

controls.criteria: Notice — the entity provides notice to data subjects about its privacy practices, updated and communicated in a timely manner whenever those practices change.
controls.notes: A living privacy notice, not a static document — it needs to actually get updated (and re-communicated) whenever what you do with personal information changes.

controls.criteria: Choice and consent — the entity communicates the choices available regarding collection, use, retention, disclosure, and disposal of personal information, and obtains consent (implicit or explicit) as required.
controls.notes: Document your basis for implicit consent where you rely on it — auditors will ask how you determined consent wasn't required to be explicit.

controls.criteria: Collection — personal information is collected consistent with the entity's privacy objectives (limited to what's necessary, obtained fairly and lawfully, from reliable sources).
controls.notes: Audit your intake forms/APIs for fields you collect but don't actually use — over-collection relative to your stated purpose is a common finding.

controls.criteria: Collection requiring explicit consent — for information requiring explicit consent, the entity communicates the need for consent and the consequences of not providing it, and obtains consent before collection.
controls.notes: Sensitive personal information needs its own explicit, informed opt-in flow — a general ToS acceptance doesn't satisfy this.

controls.criteria: Use — the entity limits use of personal information to the purposes identified in its privacy objectives.
controls.notes: Using data for a new purpose later (e.g. a new ML feature) requires going back through notice/consent again, not just proceeding because you already have the data.

controls.criteria: Retention — the entity retains personal information consistent with its privacy objectives, and protects it from erasure or destruction during the retention period.
controls.notes: A documented retention schedule with automated enforcement — "we'll delete it eventually" isn't a retention policy.

controls.criteria: Disposal — the entity securely disposes of personal information to meet its privacy objectives.
controls.notes: Deletion requests need to be captured, flagged, and actually carried out — including in backups and with any third parties the data was shared with.

controls.criteria: Access — the entity grants identified and authenticated data subjects the ability to access their stored personal information, provides copies on request, and informs them if access is denied (and why).
controls.notes: Authenticate the requester before granting access — verifying identity is part of this criterion, not just fulfilling the request quickly.

controls.criteria: Correction — the entity corrects, amends, or appends personal information based on data subject input, communicates corrections to third parties who received the original data, and explains any denial.
controls.notes: Track who you've shared data with so corrections can actually propagate downstream, not just update your own copy.

controls.criteria: Disclosure with consent — the entity discloses personal information to third parties only with the data subject's explicit consent, obtained prior to disclosure.
controls.notes: Map every third-party data flow against what your notice/consent actually covers — undisclosed sharing arrangements are a common gap here.

controls.criteria: Record of authorized disclosures — the entity creates and retains a complete, accurate, and timely record of authorized disclosures of personal information.
controls.notes: You need an actual log of who you've disclosed what to, not just a general awareness that disclosures happen.

controls.criteria: Record of unauthorized disclosures — the entity creates and retains a complete, accurate, and timely record of detected or reported unauthorized disclosures (including breaches) of personal information.
controls.notes: This record needs to exist independent of whether a breach notification threshold was triggered — track incidents even the smaller ones.

controls.criteria: Vendor privacy commitments — the entity obtains privacy commitments from vendors and third parties with access to personal information, and periodically assesses their compliance.
controls.notes: A signed DPA isn't the finish line — you need to periodically actually verify the vendor is holding up their end, not just file the contract.

controls.criteria: Vendor breach notification commitments — the entity obtains commitments from vendors to notify it of actual or suspected unauthorized disclosures, and acts on those notifications per incident-response procedures.
controls.notes: Your vendor contracts should specify a notification timeline tight enough that you can still meet your own downstream obligations after they tell you.

controls.criteria: Breach notification — the entity provides notification of breaches and incidents to affected data subjects, regulators, and others as required.
controls.notes: This overlaps directly with GDPR Art. 33/34 and CCPA's breach provisions — one notification process can usually serve multiple frameworks if built to the strictest applicable timeline.

controls.criteria: Accounting of disclosures — the entity provides data subjects with an accounting of personal information held and disclosed, upon request.
controls.notes: Similar in spirit to HIPAA's accounting-of-disclosures right and GDPR's right to know who data was shared with — needs the P6.2/P6.3 disclosure records to actually be able to answer this.

controls.criteria: Quality — the entity collects and maintains accurate, up-to-date, complete, and relevant personal information to meet its privacy objectives.
controls.notes: Periodic data-quality checks and a mechanism for data subjects to flag inaccuracies (ties back to P5.2).

controls.criteria: Monitoring and enforcement — the entity implements a process for receiving, addressing, resolving, and communicating resolution of privacy inquiries, complaints, and disputes, and periodically monitors compliance.
controls.notes: A designated privacy contact/intake channel and a documented complaint-handling process — not just "email support and hope."

items: SOC 2 — System and Organization Controls 2.
A voluntary compliance standard developed by the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA).
Focuses on service providers (or any business handling customer data in the cloud) — ensuring that data is protected through independently audited controls.

overview.title: Key Differences from Other Compliance Frameworks

items: **SOC 1** — Focuses on financial reporting controls, for orgs that impact clients' audits (e.g. payroll processors).
**SOC 2** — Broader; targets non-financial data security for tech and service firms.
**ISO 27001** — An international standard for information security management systems; more focused on ongoing implementation than a point-in-time attestation.
**SOC 2** — U.S.-centric, audit-focused, flexible in scope.
ISO 27001: an accredited body certifies that you have a structured system of policies, processes, and governance in place to manage risk on an ongoing basis.
SOC 2: an outside CPA firm tests your security controls (access management, monitoring, data handling) and issues a report saying "yes, these are working as designed."
ISO 27001 requires implementing a formal risk management process (an ISMS) and is recognized globally as a best-practice framework.
SOC 2 is an attestation report evaluating how a company's controls align with the AICPA's Trust Services Criteria — especially common in the U.S. for SaaS and cloud providers.

blocks.text: Practical framing for a client: ISO 27001 is the global seal for your whole information security management program — it shows you manage risk systematically and internationally. SOC 2 is a U.S.-centric audit that tells your customers you have the controls in place to protect their data according to industry trust standards. Many organizations pursue both — ISO for global recognition, SOC 2 for customer-facing assurance.
See the [ISO 27001 page](/frameworks/iso-27001) for the fuller picture — certification vs. attestation, and the full 93-control Annex A breakdown.

items: **GDPR/CCPA** — privacy laws with penalties for non-compliance.
**SOC 2** — voluntary, but helps demonstrate the controls that support GDPR/CCPA-style data protection requirements.
**HIPAA** — a U.S. federal law specifically governing protected health information (PHI). Mandatory (not optional) for healthcare orgs and their business associates.
**SOC 2** — a voluntary, industry-agnostic framework; not specific to healthcare, and not a substitute for HIPAA's legal requirements.

blocks.text: SOC 2 can incorporate HIPAA-aligned controls (typically via the Confidentiality or Privacy TSC categories), but it doesn't replace a HIPAA risk assessment or Business Associate Agreement (BAA) obligations. Healthcare-adjacent SaaS companies commonly pursue both: HIPAA because it's legally required once they touch PHI, and SOC 2 because enterprise customers expect it as a baseline trust signal regardless of industry.

SOC 2 is fundamentally about proving "trust" through independent audits — which is exactly why it's become close to table-stakes for B2B tech companies trying to differentiate in a competitive market.

items: **Security** — Protects systems and data from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, modification, or destruction. Common controls: logical access controls (MFA, network security, firewalls, intrusion detection, vulnerability management).

**Availability** — Ensures systems are operational and accessible as committed, minimizing downtime for users. Common controls: data backups, disaster recovery plans, uptime monitoring (e.g. 99.9% SLA guarantees).

**Processing Integrity** — Verifies that system processing is accurate, complete, and timely, without errors or manipulation. Common controls: input/output validation, error handling, automated testing.

**Confidentiality** — Safeguards sensitive information from unauthorized access throughout its lifecycle. Common controls: data classification policies, encryption in transit/at rest, secure sharing protocols.

**Privacy** — Governs the proper collection, use, retention, disclosure, and disposal of personal information (PII), in line with privacy notices and principles. Common controls: consent management, data minimization, compliance with laws like GDPR.

blocks.text: Security is mandatory for every SOC 2 report — the other four are optional and selected based on your business model (e.g. if you handle a lot of sensitive customer data, add Confidentiality).
See the [Controls tab](/frameworks/soc2/controls) for the full CC1–CC9 criteria, plus the four optional categories, in detail.

items: **Building trust and credibility** — a SOC 2 report acts as third-party validation, reducing customer due diligence and accelerating sales cycles.

**Risk reduction** — the controls SOC 2 requires directly mitigate the kinds of breaches that cost businesses an average of [$4.44 million globally in 2025](https://www.ibm.com/reports/data-breach) (IBM's Cost of a Data Breach Report).
**Competitive advantage** — many enterprises require SOC 2 from vendors; compliance opens doors to larger contracts and partnerships.
**Customers** — assured of secure data handling, especially for cloud services.
**Partners/investors** — demonstrates operational maturity, which helps in funding rounds or M&A diligence.
**Regulators** — helps align with laws like CCPA, reducing exposure to fines.

blocks.text: Quick self-score across all five Trust Services Criteria — Security plus the four optional categories. If you're answering "no" to more than half the questions in any single category, focus there before scoping an audit.

rows: Do you enforce MFA and least-privilege access?
Is data encrypted at rest and in transit?
Do new hires go through background checks and security onboarding?
Do employees complete security awareness training at least annually?
Do code/infra changes require review and approval before reaching production?
Do you run an annual risk assessment and maintain a risk register with owners?
Do you run regular vulnerability scans and patch on a defined cadence?
Do you perform internal control testing/self-audits between external audits?
Do you have a documented process for wiping/decommissioning retired assets?
Do you review the security posture of critical vendors before onboarding them?
Do you have centralized logging with alerting (SIEM or equivalent)?
Do you have a written, tabletop-tested incident response plan?
Are backups automated with defined retention, and has DR actually been tested?
Do you track uptime against a defined SLA?
Do you monitor resource capacity and scale before hitting limits?
Do you have documented data definitions/specs for what your system processes?
Are transactions validated for completeness and accuracy?
Do you have automated error handling and a defined process for reviewing exceptions?
Do you reconcile processed output against source data before delivery?
Is sensitive data classified, with access controlled accordingly?
Do you have a documented, enforced process for securely deleting/destroying confidential data?
Do you have documented PII processes and data retention/deletion policies?
Do you capture and track consent for how personal data is collected and used?
Do you have a defined process (and SLA) for handling data subject access/deletion requests?
Do you maintain and disclose a list of subprocessors handling personal data?
Can data subjects correct inaccurate personal information you hold?

items: Review policies, tech stack, and actual employee practices — not just what's written down.

[AICPA's Trust Services Criteria document](https://www.aicpa-cima.com/resources/download/2017-trust-services-criteria-with-revised-points-of-focus-2022) is the canonical resource for scoping this yourself.

blocks.text: The single longest component of the overall timeline is the **Type II observation period itself** — typically 3–12 months — because it's fixed calendar time you can't compress with more budget or headcount, unlike everything else in the process. Source: [Vanta's SOC 2 audit timeline guide](https://www.vanta.com/collection/soc-2/soc-2-audit-timeline).

Within the remediation phase specifically (the part you *can* speed up), the slowest items tend to be the ones needing new tooling or cross-team buy-in rather than just a written policy: centralized logging/SIEM, a real data classification program, and PII retention/deletion automation.

items: **MFA everywhere** — highest security impact for the lowest implementation cost, usually a config change in tools you already run.
**A formal access request/approval workflow** — auditors sample this heavily, and it's mostly process, not new tooling.
**Encryption at rest and in transit** — often just flipping on settings already available in your cloud infrastructure.
**A written incident response plan** — cheap to draft, and required regardless of maturity level.

Once those are in place, move to the harder infrastructure/process work — centralized logging, a defined vulnerability-scanning cadence, and data classification — since those take the longest and benefit from starting early.

blocks.text: Achieving SOC 2 typically takes 3–12 months, depending on where you're starting from.

items: **Scoping** — define system boundaries and select which TSC categories apply (1–2 weeks).
**Gap analysis** — assess current controls against the criteria (about 1 month).
**Remediation** — implement fixes for identified gaps (2–6 months).
**Readiness assessment** — a mock audit / internal review of controls after remediation.
**Actual audit / report issuance** — the CPA firm's fieldwork, then you receive and can share the report.
Startups: roughly 6–9 months for a Type 2 report.
Mature orgs with existing controls: 3–6 months — but factor in the required observation period for Type 2 (often ~12 months of evidence).
**Type 1** — a snapshot of control design at a single point in time. Quicker but less comprehensive (2–3 months total).
**Type 2** — evaluates control effectiveness over an observation window (e.g. 6–12 months). The gold standard for customers.

blocks.text: Select an [AICPA-licensed CPA firm](https://www.aicpa-cima.com/search/SOC+2) to perform the audit, and prepare by organizing evidence ahead of time. Fieldwork typically involves interviews and control testing — expect 4–8 weeks.
The most common findings are inadequate logging and unpatched systems. Most are fixable with quick remediation and a retest before the report is finalized.
Compliance is ongoing, not a one-time event.
Quarterly internal audits, plus continuous control testing via automation where possible.
Reports need to be updated yearly; bridge letters can cover gaps between formal audits.
As you grow, expand scope (e.g. add Privacy when you launch a feature that touches more PII), and integrate control checks into DevOps for automated evidence collection.
